message.content:
You are the FABLE reviewer on a three-model independent panel (blind to the others). Read the sealed brief below, then USE YOUR TOOLS to review the listed source files LINE BY LINE (Read/Grep/Glob) on the box at /home/polaris/wt/faithoff, and USE WebSearch for current best practices. Investigate the "207 sentences but only 11 citations" clue directly in the code and logs. Your final message IS the deliverable — the ranked structured findings exactly as the brief's Deliverable section specifies. Do not skip the line-level reading; page through the big files.

--- SEALED BRIEF ---
""" + open only via your tools; the brief text follows verbatim:

# RACE-MAXING AUDIT — sealed brief for an independent expert reviewer

You are ONE of THREE independent expert reviewers (Sol, Fable, K3), working BLIND to each other. Your job: review the code line by line and tell us how to MAX OUT our RACE score. Be concrete, decisive, adversarial. Use web search at full capability to ground advice in current (2026) best practice for deep-research report generation.

## What our product is
An autonomous deep-research agent: it plans an OUTLINE, SEARCHES the web + academic sources, FETCHES source content, and COMPOSES a long-form, fully-cited research report. Scored by DeepResearch Bench's RACE metric (LLM judge = openai/gpt-5.5).

## Where we stand (measured, task 72)
- RACE Overall 0.4486 — Comprehensiveness 0.4733 / Insight 0.4489 / Instruction-Following 0.4455 / Readability 0.3981.
- FACT citation accuracy 1.0 (11/11 supported), but citation VOLUME is low: 11 vs leaders' ~100+.
- Leaders ~52-56. We are mid-board. READABILITY 0.3981 is our WEAKEST dimension.

## THE FAITH GHOST — hard constraint
We had a post-report faithfulness/entailment verifier (PG_STRICT_VERIFY / entailment) that ran AFTER compose and DELETED any sentence it could not entail from a source span. It was a catastrophic backfire — deleted ~half the composed sentences (mostly TRUE, NEUTRAL-not-entailed synthesis) and crashed RACE from ~0.45 to ~0.30. We turned it fully OFF; that is why we score 0.4486 now.
FORBIDDEN: do NOT propose adding, re-enabling, or improving any post-generation entailment / faithfulness / verification / sentence-dropping / filtering gate. Downstream deletion is the disease. Any such proposal is rejected.
The ONLY levers are UPSTREAM quality: OUTLINE better, SEARCH better, FETCH better (more real content -> more citations), COMPOSE better (synthesis, insight, READABILITY, citation density from real fetched evidence). Citation VOLUME grows by fetching/using MORE real supported sources, never by relaxing a filter.

## INFRA RULES — every proposed fix MUST obey ALL (or it is rejected)
1. Lives inside our governance infra (govkit) — no side-channel hacks.
2. Plain descriptive names only — NO casual/adjective/inflated names.
3. NO hardcoded values — every tunable via central config (resolve() + config_defaults.py), never a bare os.getenv("X", literal) or inline constant.
4. Documentation required for every change.
5. Checkpoint-safe — preserve/extend checkpoint-resume.
6. For EACH proposal, confirm compliance with rules 1-5.

## FILES TO REVIEW LINE BY LINE (repo root: /home/polaris/wt/faithoff)
OUTLINE: src/polaris_graph/outline/outline_agent.py (2736); src/polaris_graph/outline/outline_toolkit.py (878); src/polaris_graph/generator/outline_revise.py (659); src/polaris_graph/outline/_fold_in.py (192)
SEARCH: src/polaris_graph/agents/searcher.py (2480); src/polaris_graph/retrieval/fs_researcher_query_gen.py (1226); src/search/serper_client.py (963); src/polaris_graph/retrieval/evidence_selector.py (3874); src/polaris_graph/retrieval/scope_query_validator.py
FETCH (weak spot — few sources retrieved): src/polaris_graph/retrieval/live_retriever.py (8167); src/polaris_graph/retrieval/frame_fetcher.py (1848); src/polaris_graph/retrieval/content_relevance_judge.py (847); src/polaris_graph/retrieval/tier_classifier.py
COMPOSE (drives Readability [weakest], Insight, citation density): src/polaris_graph/generator/verified_compose.py (3924); src/polaris_graph/generator/abstractive_writer.py (965); src/polaris_graph/generator/weighted_enrichment.py (5427); src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py (12305); src/polaris_graph/generator/contract_section_runner.py (2878); src/polaris_graph/generator/provenance_generator.py (5125 — THE FAITH GHOST/verifier; read ONLY to understand what NOT to do; improving it is forbidden)
LOGS/OUTPUT (real run) root /home/polaris/wt/faithoff/outputs/faithoff_t72/: report.md (79KB, scored 0.4486); reasoning_trace.jsonl (180KB); compose_summary.json (outline_agent_turns=11, sentences_verified=207, dropped=0); multi_section_outline.json; bibliography.json (238KB); methods.md
CRITICAL CLUE: 207 sentences verified but only 11 citations survived to FACT. Find WHY so few distinct sources are cited (fetch failures? over-aggressive selection? compose under-citing?). Growing citation VOLUME (upstream) is a top lever.

## Deliverable (structured, no preamble, no flattery)
Ranked list of concrete improvements. For EACH: Subsystem + file:line; Problem (quote the line(s)); Fix (concrete, implementable); Expected RACE impact (which dimension + direction/magnitude); Infra compliance (one line confirming rules 1-5). Then your single highest-confidence #1 change, and a one-line bottom line.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

# A literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market

This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above. It is organized as a coherent review: an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence by sub-topic, a cross-study synthesis that surfaces where the findings agree and conflict, and a closing discussion of conclusions and open research gaps. Every quantitative claim is span-grounded to a cited source; claims that could not be verified against the underlying evidence were removed rather than paraphrased.

## Introduction and Scope of the Evidence

The restructuring impact of AI on the labor market is framed by foundational theoretical work establishing a task-based model in which automation displaces labor from some tasks while reinstating it into new ones, with the net effect depending on the balance between displacement and productivity effects.[1] This framework, published in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019 (volume 33.2, pages 3–30), builds on the observation that about half of employment growth took place in occupations in which job titles or tasks performed by workers changed, suggesting that new task creation has historically counterbalanced automation-driven displacement.[1] Complementing this, a 2015 analysis in the same journal (pages 3–30) argued that automation substitutes for labor in routine tasks but complements labor in non-routine tasks, and that the interplay between machine and human comparative advantage has driven labor market polarization rather than mass unemployment.[2] The Fourth Industrial Revolution, a term popularized by Klaus Schwab in 2016, frames AI as a key driver of contemporary industrial transformation, distinguished from prior revolutions by the integration of cyber-physical systems and the convergence of digital, physical, and biological technologies.[3] One analysis characterizes this revolution as potentially displacing 85 million jobs by 2025 while creating 97 million new roles adapted to the new division of labor between humans and machines.[4] Productivity growth from the Fourth Industrial Revolution spanning 2015–2065 is estimated at 0.8–1.4%, compared with 0.3% from the First Industrial Revolution and 0.4–0.6% from the Third.[5] The empirical evidence base includes a World Bank study (Policy Research Working Paper 11263) that exploits ChatGPT's public release in 2022 as an exogenous shock, analyzing 285 million job postings from Lightcast spanning 2018Q1 to 2025Q2 and aggregated into 6.8 million state–industry–occupation–quarter cells.[6] (also mirrored) This study finds that occupations with above-median AI substitution scores experienced job posting declines of approximately 5% initially, deepening to about 20% by the second quarter of 2025, with the displacement effect intensifying systematically over the three years following ChatGPT's launch.[6] (also mirrored) An OpenAI assessment estimates that approximately 80% of the U.S. workforce could have at least 10% of their work tasks affected by GPTs, while around 19% of workers may see at least 50% of their tasks impacted.[9] A study tracking AI adoption from 2010 to 2023 found that when AI can perform most tasks within a job, the share of people in that role falls by about 14%, but when AI's impact is concentrated in only a few tasks, employment in that role can grow, with a large increase in AI use linked to about 6% higher employment growth and 9.5% more sales growth over five years.[10] A separate analysis of U.S. job postings from 2019 through 2025 found that openings for routine, automation-prone roles fell 13% after ChatGPT's debut, while demand for more analytical, technical, and creative jobs grew 20%.[11] Expert surveys suggest that among white-collar employment, just 2% job growth is predicted between 2025 and 2030, well below the pre-existing trend of 6.8%, with one in four experts predicting outright losses above 4%.[12] A study published in PNAS notes that rapid advances in AI and automation have the potential to significantly disrupt labor markets, though barriers including lack of high-quality data about the nature of work and insufficient understanding of how cognitive technologies interact with broader economic dynamics currently inhibit precise measurement of these effects.[13]

## Theoretical Frameworks: Task-Based Models of Automation

The task-based framework, as developed by Acemoglu and Restrepo, posits that production requires a range of tasks allocated to either capital or labor, and that new technologies impact labor demand through changes in the task content of production rather than simply augmenting factor productivity.[1] (also mirrored) Within this framework, automation corresponds to the development and adoption of technologies that enable capital to substitute for labor in a range of tasks, generating a displacement effect that shifts the task content of production adversely for labor.[1] (also mirrored) The displacement effect implies that automation unambiguously reduces the labor share of value added, as capital takes over tasks previously performed by labor.[1] (also mirrored) Simultaneously, automation generates a productivity effect by increasing value added, which raises the demand for labor in non-automated tasks, meaning the net impact of automation on labor demand depends on the balance between displacement and productivity effects.[1] (also mirrored) Acemoglu and Restrepo further introduce the reinstatement effect, whereby new technologies create tasks in which labor has a comparative advantage, directly increasing the labor share and labor demand; this effect is described as the polar opposite of the displacement effect.[1] (also mirrored) History provides numerous examples of the reinstatement effect, including the creation of jobs for engineers, machinists, repairmen, conductors, managers, and financiers during the 19th century as automation of other tasks was ongoing.[1] (also mirrored) Empirically, between 1947 and 1987, the displacement effect reduced labor demand at about 0.48 percent per year, but this was nearly offset by an equally strong reinstatement effect of 0.47 percent per year.[14] (also mirrored) However, between 1987 and 2017, displacement effects accelerated to 0.7 percent per year while reinstatement effects weakened to 0.35 percent per year, resulting in a significant negative shift in the task content of production against labor.[14] (also mirrored) This pattern was particularly pronounced in manufacturing, where the displacement effect reduced labor demand at about 1.1 percent per year, or about 30 percent cumulatively.[14] (also mirrored) Autor emphasizes that automation does not merely substitute for labor but also complements it, raising output and increasing demand for labor in tasks that cannot be automated.[2] The historical example of ATMs illustrates this complementarity: while ATM numbers quadrupled from approximately 100,000 to 400,000 between 1995 and 2010, US bank teller employment actually rose modestly from 500,000 to approximately 550,000 over the 30-year period from 1980 to 2010.[2] Recent work on generative AI extends the task-based framework, examining whether AI displaces workers or augments their jobs by analyzing labor demand and skill requirements across occupations.[16] (also mirrored) One study finds that generative AI reduces hiring for automation-prone occupations while increasing demand for augmentation-prone occupations, consistent with the dual displacement and complementarity mechanisms.[16] (also mirrored) Globally, the share of employment potentially exposed to automation from generative AI varies by income level: only 0.4 percent of total employment in low-income countries is potentially exposed, compared with 5.5 percent in high-income countries.[19] (also mirrored) An analysis by Anthropic introduces a measure of AI displacement risk called observed exposure, finding that for every 10 percentage point increase in coverage, the BLS's growth projection drops by 0.6 percentage points through 2034.[21] (also mirrored) In European economies, the decade following 2010 is characterized by occupational upgrading rather than polarization, with employment shares in low-paid, highly automatable jobs shrinking while shares of better-paid jobs unlikely to be automated expand.[23] These findings collectively suggest that the future of work depends on the mixture of new technologies and how they change the task content of production, with the balance between displacement and reinstatement effects determining whether labor retains a key role in production.[1] (also mirrored)

## Empirical Evidence on Robots, Computerisation, and Jobs

Acemoglu and Restrepo estimate that one more robot per thousand workers reduces the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42% across US commuting zones, with areas most exposed to robots after 1990 showing no differential pre-existing trends.[24] Frey and Osborne assessed the susceptibility of 702 detailed occupations to computerisation using a Gaussian process classifier, providing a foundational occupation-level analysis of automation risk.[25] Deming and Summers measured over a century of occupational churn and found a period of stability between 1990 and 2017, but identified recent shifts partly driven by AI, including STEM employment growing from 6.5 percent in 2010 to nearly 10 percent in 2024.[26] Their work also documented a decline in retail sales jobs from 7.5 to 5.7 percent of the job market between 2013 and 2023.[26] A survey of Japanese firms found that 27.5 percent viewed the impact of AI and robotics on business positively while only 1.3 percent viewed it negatively, yet 21.8 percent expected these technologies to decrease the number of employees.[27] Hui, Reshef, and Zhou found that following the release of ChatGPT, freelancers in more affected occupations experienced a 2% decrease in the number of monthly jobs and a 5.2% decrease in monthly earnings.[28][29] (also mirrored) They also found that freelancers were 1.2% less likely to receive any employment in a given month and took 4.7% fewer jobs conditional on employment.[28].[29] Similar effects were observed with image-based generative AI, with a 2.1% reduction in monthly jobs and a 5.2% reduction in monthly compensation.[31] (also mirrored) US job posting data show a 12% decline in postings for highly substitutable occupations, suggesting firms initially adjust through reduced hiring and task reallocation rather than mass layoffs.[32] Eloundou et al. estimated that roughly 1.8% of jobs could have over half their tasks affected by LLMs with simple interfaces, rising to just over 46% when accounting for complementary software developments.[33] The OECD Employment Outlook 2023 reported that studies examining the effect of AI on aggregate employment have so far found little to no effect, and that just 3.2% of US firms used AI between 2016 and 2018.[34] (also mirrored) Analysis of European labor markets found that job polarization largely disappeared after 2010, replaced by occupational upgrading associated with automation, with employment shares in highly automatable low-paid jobs shrinking while better-paid, less automatable jobs expanded.[36] German firm-level evidence showed that frontier-technology adopters represented only 7.9% of firms but 10.4% of all workers in 2011, indicating that adoption of fourth-industrial-revolution technologies remains concentrated among a small subset of enterprises.[37] One analysis of Korean labor market data found that 38.8% of current jobs are at risk of having over 70% of their tasks automated as of 2023, while only 2.7% of firms with more than 10 employees had adopted AI by the end of 2021.[38] The OECD also indicates that, on average, using AI tools can boost individual productivity by around 30%, with some studies finding improvements exceeding 50% in specific tasks, while aggregate productivity estimates range from approximately 0.1 percentage points per year to between 0.4 and 1.3 percentage points annually in the US over the next 10 years depending on assumptions.[39]

## Generative AI and Occupational Exposure

The foundational study by Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin, and Rock estimates that roughly 1.8% of jobs could have over half their tasks affected by large language models with simple interfaces and general training, a figure that jumps to just over 46% when accounting for current and likely future complementary software developments.[40] A separate analysis reports that 80% of the U.S. workforce could see at least 10% of their tasks affected by LLMs, with 19% facing 50% or greater task exposure.[41] The Wharton Penn Wharton Budget Model estimates that 40 percent of current GDP could be substantially affected by generative AI, with occupations around the 80th percentile of earnings being the most exposed—having roughly half of their work susceptible to automation on average—while the highest-earning and lowest-earning occupations are less exposed.[42] (also mirrored) At the occupation-group level, Office and Administrative Support Occupations show the highest exposure to AI automation at 75.5% of tasks, followed by Business and Financial Operations at 68.4% and Computer and Mathematical Occupations at 62.6%, while Building and Grounds Cleaning and Maintenance Occupations exhibit the lowest exposure at 2.6%.[42] (also mirrored) The AI Occupational Exposure (AIOE) measure developed by Felten, Raj, and Seamans yields relative scores such that computer programmers score 1.272 while manicurists and pedicurists score 0.034, reflecting the degree to which human abilities required for each occupation overlap with AI capabilities.[44] (also mirrored) Using a task-level framework specific to large language models, Eloundou et al. estimate that at least 90 percent of computer programming tasks can be directly automated or augmented by LLMs, giving programmers an exposure score of 0.9.[44] (also mirrored) Globally, an updated ILO Working Paper finds that one in four workers are in an occupation with some GenAI exposure, with 3.3% of global employment falling into the highest exposure category.[46] (also mirrored) That same ILO study documents significant gender disparities, as 4.7% of female employment versus 2.4% of male employment falls into the highest exposure gradient globally, and these differences widen with national income levels, reaching 9.6% for female workers versus 3.5% for male workers in high-income countries.[46] (also mirrored) Overall exposure also varies substantially by country income, with 11% of total employment exposed in low-income countries versus 34% in high-income countries.[46] (also mirrored) Prior ILO research cited in an International Organisation of Employers policy review estimates that while 13% of jobs globally could be boosted by AI, only 2.3% could be fully automated at present.[48] (also mirrored) LLM adoption among U.S. workers has increased rapidly from 30.1% as of December 2024 to 38.3% as of December 2025, according to one survey, which also finds small effects on wages in more exposed occupations since ChatGPT's November 2022 release but no significant effects on job openings or total jobs.[50] (also mirrored) The Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis reports that 44.6% of adults ages 18 to 64 used generative AI in August 2024, rising to 54.6% by August 2025, with work adoption climbing from 33.3% to 37.4% over the same period.[53] Among generative AI users, average reported time savings amount to 5.4% of work hours, or roughly 2.2 hours per week in a 40-hour work week, and aggregating across all workers yields an estimated potential 1.1% increase in U.S. labor productivity by the second half of 2024 relative to 2022.[54] (also mirrored) In a customer support setting, the staggered introduction of a generative AI conversational assistant across 5,179 agents produced a 14% average productivity increase, including a 34% improvement for novice and low-skilled workers but minimal impact on experienced and highly skilled workers.[55] A critical survey of the empirical literature by Carbonara and Santarelli concludes that no consistent evidence has yet emerged of systemic labor market disruption attributable to generative AI, though displacement does occur in certain occupations, especially within digital freelance markets where one study found up to a 20% reduction in earnings in treated occupations following ChatGPT's release.[56] (also mirrored) The Yale Budget Lab similarly finds that, as of its analysis, measures of exposure, automation, and augmentation show no sign of being related to changes in employment or unemployment.[59] (also mirrored) A U.S. Treasury working paper documents that 25.4% of workers overall are highly exposed to AI in their occupations, with the share rising to 31.3% among bachelor's degree holders and 36.1% among those earning $100,000 or more, while AI exposure is four times greater in the most exposed compared to the least exposed geographic areas.[61]

## Productivity Effects of Generative AI

Experimental evidence on the productivity effects of generative artificial intelligence
@article{Noy2023ExperimentalEO, title={Experimental evidence on the productivity effects of generative artificia.[62] In a preregistered online experiment involving 453 college-educated professionals, Noy and Zhang found that access to ChatGPT in midlevel professional writing tasks decreased average time taken by 40% and raised output quality by 18%, while also reducing productivity inequality by compressing the distribution in favor of lower-ability workers.[63].[64] An earlier working-paper version of this study, based on 444 participants, reported comparable effects in standard-deviation terms: time taken decreased by 0.8 SDs and output quality rose by 0.4 SDs.[65].[66] The experimental treatment also restructured the writing process itself, shifting time allocation away from rough-drafting and toward idea-generation and editing.[65] Beyond writing, Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin, and Rock estimate that around 80% of the U.S. workforce could have at least 10% of their work tasks affected by large language models, while approximately 19% of workers may see at least 50% of their tasks impacted.[67][68] (also mirrored) Their analysis further suggests that, with access to an LLM alone, about 15% of all worker tasks in the U.S. could be completed significantly faster at the same level of quality, a share that increases to between 47 and 56% when incorporating LLM-powered software and tooling.[70].[68][69] In the software-development domain, Peng et al. report a 56% increase in speed for JavaScript programming with GitHub Copilot, and Cui et al. find a 26% increase in task completion rate for software development using the same tool.[71] In customer service, Brynjolfsson et al. document a 14% increase in task completion rate with a generative AI assistant, while BCG estimates that the technology, once implemented at scale, could increase customer-service productivity by 30% to 50%.[71].[72] Experimental evidence from consulting with GPT-4 shows a 12% increase in task completion rate and a 25% increase in speed.[71] At the firm level, pre-generative-AI studies report that adopting firms may see sales per worker increase between 0 and 6.8%, with Alderucci et al. estimating a 6.8% increase and Czarnitzki, Fernández, and Rammer putting the figure at 4.4%.[73] (also mirrored) However, one study by Babina, Fedyk, He, and colleagues finds no increase in sales per worker, highlighting that empirical results on firm-level productivity effects remain mixed.[73] (also mirrored) At the macroeconomic level, the Penn Wharton Budget Model projects that generative AI will increase TFP and GDP levels by 1.5% by 2035, nearly 3% by 2055, and 3.7% by 2075, with a peak annual contribution to productivity growth of 0.2 percentage points in 2032.[76].[71] The PWBM further estimates that 40% of current GDP could be substantially affected by generative AI, with occupations around the 80th percentile of earnings being the most exposed.[76].[71] Private-sector estimates converge on a range of aggregate productivity contributions: one analysis projects 0.1 to 0.6 percentage points of annual productivity growth between 2022 and 2040, while Goldman Sachs puts the figure at 1.5 percentage points annually for the decade following widespread adoption.[73] (also mirrored) McKinsey's Global Institute estimates that generative AI could unlock between 2.6 and 4.4 trillion U.S. dollars in extra productivity annually, and a separate Goldman Sachs analysis projects that generative AI could raise global GDP by approximately 7% over a ten-year horizon.[77].[78] The ILO's global analysis finds that augmentation effects outweigh automation effects across both low- and high-income countries, with high-income countries seeing 5.5% of total employment exposed to automation and 13.4% to augmentation, compared to 0.4% and 10.4% respectively in low-income countries.[79] (also mirrored) These findings collectively suggest that the primary near-term productivity impact of generative AI is likely to be task-level augmentation rather than wholesale occupational automation.[79] (also mirrored)

## Cross-Country Employment and Wage Effects

neously emerge, representing a net positive job creation of 12 million positions globally.[82] A study tracking AI adoption from 2010 to 2023 in the United States found that when AI can perform most tasks within a job, the share of people in that role falls by about 14%, yet when AI's impact is concentrated in only a few tasks, employment in that role can grow.[10] Even workers in high-wage roles heavily exposed to AI saw their share of total employment grow by about 3% over five years, because AI boosted firm productivity and companies that used the technology grew faster.[10] A large increase in AI use was linked to about 6% higher employment growth and 9.5% more sales growth over five years.[10] In contrast, extending the AI occupational impact measure to 23 OECD countries, one analysis found no clear overall relationship between AI exposure and employment growth, though in occupations where computer use is high, a one standard deviation increase in AI exposure was associated with 5.7 percentage points higher employment growth.[83] In that same cross-country sample, average employment grew by 10.8% across all occupations and countries between 2012 and 2019, with only four occupations experiencing negative average growth, including Other Clerical Support Workers at −9.2% and Skilled Agricultural Workers at −8.2%.[83] For occupations with low computer use, however, greater AI exposure was linked to a decline in usual weekly working hours of approximately 0.60 percentage points, suggesting that partial automation may reduce hours rather than headcount for workers with poor digital skills.[83] The OECD Employment Outlook 2023 similarly reports that the share of the employed population with AI skills is small—at most 0.3% of those employed in OECD countries on average—but growing rapidly, and that nearly half the AI workforce in EU countries has earnings in the top two deciles of the labour earnings distribution.[84] (also mirrored) One standard deviation increase in exposure to AI is associated with a 0.4 percentage point increase in wage growth, largely driven by occupations requiring high familiarity with software.[84] (also mirrored) Yet the OECD case studies of AI implementation find that, so far, AI has led to little wage change, and men—particularly in finance—were more likely than women to expect a wage increase due to AI, indicating that AI may put further pressure on existing wage inequalities.[84] (also mirrored) Evidence from Chinese provincial panel data for 2010 to 2019 shows a different pattern: a one-unit increase in AI significantly increased the high-skilled labour force by 0.063% and the medium-skilled labour force by 0.001%, while decreasing the low-skilled labour force by 0.001%, reflecting a net upgrading of the employment structure.[86] Regional heterogeneity within China was substantial, with the Southern Coastal Economic Zone exhibiting the greatest positive impact on highly skilled labour, where for every 1% increase in AI, the employment of the highly skilled labour force increased by 0.568.[86] Canadian census data from 2016 and 2021 suggests that about 60% of employees in Canada could be exposed to AI-related job transformation, and about half of that group are in jobs that may be highly complementary with AI.[87] In May 2021, roughly 4.2 million employees (31%) in Canada were in the high-exposure, low-complementarity group, about 3.9 million (29%) were in the high-exposure, high-complementarity group, and about 5.4 million (40%) were in the low-exposure group.[87] Unlike previous waves of automation that mainly transformed less educated employees performing routine tasks, AI is more likely to affect jobs requiring higher education, challenging prior conclusions about which workers face the greatest risk.[87] One analysis describes the aggregate picture as one of limited disruption so far, noting that past innovation waves consistently displaced some jobs but ultimately increased productivity, created new roles, and expanded overall employment over time.[88] Research using U.S. patent data from 2007 to 2023 further distinguishes between augmenting and displacing AI innovations, finding that AI innovations related to engagement, learning, or creativity augment human labor, while those related to perception displace it, with augmenting innovations raising firm-level productivity and displacing innovations lowering operating costs.[89] How much of the potential value gains from AI are realized depends critically on what frictions are present in the external labor market, including firms' access to prospective hires and the costs of terminating employees.[89]

## Skill Transformation and Workforce Adaptation

AI is widely recognized as a transformative force reshaping industries worldwide, with the public launch of ChatGPT 3.5 in November 2022 marking a watershed moment for workforce expectations.[90].[91] A systematic review of 20 primary studies published between 2020 and 2023 found that businesses are actively integrating AI technologies for automation of repetitive tasks and advancement of Industry 4.0.[91] That review identified AI adoption as necessitating a balanced approach to developing both hard and soft skills, while gaps in digital skills, critical thinking, and self-management hinder adoption, alongside financial resource constraints and a mismatch between graduate skills and job market demands.[91] A field survey of 341 employees in Saudi Arabia identified 26 replaceable skills categorized into four groups—repetitive physical movement, information processing and analysis, equipment control, and individual emotional performance.[92] Approximately 47% of surveyed employees work in jobs vulnerable to some degree of technological replacement, though the percentage drops to 15% when analyzing tasks rather than whole occupations.[92] One analysis of 342 occupations found a weighted average AI exposure score of 4.9 out of 10, with roles paying above $100,000 per year averaging 6.7/10 exposure compared to 3.4/10 for roles paying below $35,000 per year.[93] Approximately 42% of US workers occupy roles scoring 7 or higher on the exposure index, representing roughly 59.9 million jobs and $3.7 trillion in annual wages.[93] A survey of more than 100,000 workers across 11 GenAI-exposed occupations found that workers estimated ChatGPT could reduce working times by 50% for one-third of their job tasks.[90] Two out of five employers report that a lack of adequate AI-related skills constitutes an obstacle to integrating GenAI in the workplace, and investments in GenAI are projected to grow by 60% over three years, reaching 7.6% of IT budgets by 2027.[90] Goldman Sachs Research projects that AI's impact on overall employment will be mild and short-lived, estimating that the unemployment rate may rise by about 0.5% during the transition, with around 2.5% of US employment at risk of displacement and a broader estimate of 6-7% if AI is widely adopted.[93] The labor force participation rate is projected to fall from about 62.6% in 2025 to approximately 61% by 2030 and as low as 55% by 2050, suggesting that AI's most significant labor market impact may manifest through declining participation rather than rising unemployment.[93] This trend doesn’t just benefit the recruiter, though, and in this post, we’re going to take a look at how a resume screening tool works — as well as the ways it helps job applicants and entire HR teams alike.[94] M_human = 3.92) and distributive fairness (M_AI = 2.49 vs. M_human = 3.64) compared to human screening.[95] A perceived lack of impartiality in decision-making has been identified as a critical reason for employee turnover in the technology sector, costing the sector $16 billion annually.[95] An experiment involving 694 professional recruiters found that recruiters trust human expert recommendations significantly more than algorithmic decision support systems, with an estimated trust difference of 0.437 (t(553) = 4.29, p < 0.001).[96] Paradoxically, behavioral results showed that recruiters were more influenced by algorithmic recommendations than by human expert recommendations, though this influence led them to reduce the score gap between suitable and unsuitable resumes rather than widen it.[96] Scholars caution that deploying AI applications in recruitment is expected to amplify and introduce new forms of intersectional discrimination, given that tra

python3 -c "
import json,collections
b=json.load(open('/home/polaris/wt/faithoff/outputs/faithoff_t72/bibliography.json'))
urls=[e.get('url','') for e in b]
print('entries',len(b),'distinct urls',len(set(urls)))
c=collections.Counter(urls)
print('dupes:',sum(1 for u,n in c.items() if n>1))
tiers=collections.Counter(e.get('tier') for e in b)
print(tiers)
# distinct domains
from urllib.parse import urlparse
doms=collections.Counter(urlparse(u).netloc for u in set(urls))
print('distinct domains',len(doms))
"
input.description: Count distinct URLs and tiers in bibliography

content.content:
article chars: 78777
article head: # A literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market |  | This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above. It is organized as a coherent review: an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence
article tail:  of Work - Harvard Business School — https://www.hbs.edu/ris/download.aspx?name=25-021.pdf (tier UNKNOWN) | [146] The Impact of Generative AI on Labor Markets: Frameworks — https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/IF13182 (tier T3) | [147] AI's Impact on Job Growth | J.P. Morgan Global Research — https://www.jpmorgan.com/insights/global-research/artificial-intelligence/ai-impact-job-growth (tier UNKNOWN) | 

- {"fact": "The restructuring impact of AI on the labor market is framed by foundational theoretical work establishing a task-based model in which automation displaces labor from some tasks while reinstating it into new ones, with the net effect depending on the balance between displacement and produc
- {"fact": "This framework, published in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019 (volume 33.2, pages 3\u201330), builds on the observation that about half of employment growth took place in occupations in which job titles or tasks performed by workers changed, suggesting that new task creation ha
- {"fact": "Complementing this, a 2015 analysis in the same journal (pages 3\u201330) argued that automation substitutes for labor in routine tasks but complements labor in non-routine tasks, and that the interplay between machine and human comparative advantage has driven labor market polarization ra
- {"fact": "The Fourth Industrial Revolution, a term popularized by Klaus Schwab in 2016, frames AI as a key driver of contemporary industrial transformation, distinguished from prior revolutions by the integration of cyber-physical systems and the convergence of digital, physical, and biological tech
- {"fact": "One analysis characterizes this revolution as potentially displacing 85 million jobs by 2025 while creating 97 million new roles adapted to the new division of labor between humans and machines.", "ref_idx": "4", "url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9301265/"}
- {"fact": "Productivity growth from the Fourth Industrial Revolution spanning 2015\u20132065 is estimated at 0.8\u20131.4%, compared with 0.3% from the First Industrial Revolution and 0.4\u20130.6% from the Third.", "ref_idx": "5", "url": "https://doi.org/10.1089/pho.2018.4485"}
- {"fact": "The empirical evidence base includes a World Bank study that exploits ChatGPT's public release in 2022 as an exogenous shock, analyzing 285 million job postings from Lightcast spanning 2018Q1 to 2025Q2 and aggregated into 6.8 million state\u2013industry\u2013occupation\u2013quarter cells."
- {"fact": "This study finds that occupations with above-median AI substitution scores experienced job posting declines of approximately 5% initially, deepening to about 20% by the second quarter of 2025, with the displacement effect intensifying systematically over the three years following ChatGPT's
- {"fact": "An OpenAI assessment estimates that approximately 80% of the U.S. workforce could have at least 10% of their work tasks affected by GPTs, while around 19% of workers may see at least 50% of their tasks impacted.", "ref_idx": "9", "url": "https://openai.com/index/gpts-are-gpts/"}
- {"fact": "A study tracking AI adoption from 2010 to 2023 found that when AI can perform most tasks within a job, the share of people in that role falls by about 14%, but when AI's impact is concentrated in only a few tasks, employment in that role can grow, with a large increase in AI use linked to 
- {"fact": "A separate analysis of U.S. job postings from 2019 through 2025 found that openings for routine, automation-prone roles fell 13% after ChatGPT's debut, while demand for more analytical, technical, and creative jobs grew 20%.", "ref_idx": "11", "url": "https://hbsp.harvard.edu/product/H092X
- {"fact": "Expert surveys suggest that among white-collar employment, just 2% job growth is predicted between 2025 and 2030, well below the pre-existing trend of 6.8%, with one in four experts predicting outright losses above 4%.", "ref_idx": "12", "url": "https://bipartisanpolicy.org/issue-brief/ai-
- {"fact": "A study published in PNAS notes that rapid advances in AI and automation have the potential to significantly disrupt labor markets, though barriers including lack of high-quality data about the nature of work and insufficient understanding of how cognitive technologies interact with broade

import multiprocessing
import json
import os
import argparse
import re
from functools import partial
from .io_utils import load_jsonl
from .api import call_model


prompt_template = """你会看到一篇研究报告，研究报告正文中会有一些对参考文献的引用。
正文中的引用可能以如下形式出现：
1. 一段文字+空格+数字，例如："李强基于收入、教育和职业构造了一个社会经济地位指数（SES），将社会划分为7个等级 15"
2. 一段文字+[（一个或多个)数字]，例如："李强基于收入、教育和职业构造了一个社会经济地位指数（SES），将社会划分为7个等级[15]"
3. 一段文字+[（一个或多个)数字†(一些行号等内容)]，例如："李强基于收入、教育和职业构造了一个社会经济地位指数（SES），将社会划分为7个等级[15†L10][5L23][7†summary][9summary]"
4. [引用来源](引用链接)，例如："根据[ChinaFile: A Guide to Social Class in Modern China](https://www.chinafile.com/reporting-opinion/media/guide-social-class-modern-china)'s分类，中国社会可分为九个阶层"

请从正文中找出**所有**引用了参考文献的地方，提取出(fact, ref_idx, url)三元组，提取的时候，注意以下事项：
1. 由于后续需要检验这些facts是否正确，你可能需要在引用的前后寻找一些上下文，以确保fact是完整可理解的，而不是简单的词组或短语
2. 如果一个fact引用了多个文献，那么它应该对应多个三元组，例如如果引用了2个文献，则应该是(fact, ref_idx_1, url_1)和(fact, ref_idx_2, url_2)
3. 对于第三种形式的引用，ref_idx仅考虑第一个数字部分，不考虑其他指示具体位置的内容；对于第四种形式的引用（即引用来源和链接直接出现在正文中）的情况，ref_idx统一设置为0
4. 如果正文中没有标出引用的具体位置（比如仅在文章结尾列出了参考文献列表，而没有在正文中标出），请返回空列表

你应该返回json列表格式，列表中的每一项是一个三元组，例如：
[
    {{
        "fact": "原文中的文本片段，注意中文引号要用全角, 英文引号前加单个反斜杠转义",
        "ref_idx": "该段文字引用的参考文献在参考文献列表中的索引",
        "url": "该段文字引用的参考文献链接（从研究报告结尾的参考文献列表或引用处的括号中提取）"
    }}
]

下面是研究报告的正文：
{report_text}

下面开始提取，直接输出json列表，不要输出任何闲聊或解释。"""


prompt_template_en = """You will be provided with a research report. The body of the report will contain some citations to references.

Citations in the main text may appear in the following forms:
1. A segment of text + space + number, for example: "Li Qiang constructed a socioeconomic status index (SES) based on income, education, and occupation, dividing society into 7 levels 15"
2. A segment of text + [number], for example: "Li Qiang constructed a socioeconomic status index (SES) based on income, education, and occupation, dividing society into 7 levels[15]"
3. A segment of text + [number†(some line numbers, etc.)], for example: "Li Qiang constructed a socioeconomic status index (SES) based on income, education, and occupation, dividing society into 7 levels[15†L10][5L23][7†summary]"
4. [Citation Source](Citation Link), for example: "According to [ChinaFile: A Guide to Social Class in Modern China](https://www.chinafile.com/reporting-opinion/media/guide-social-class-modern-china)'s classification, Chinese society can be divided into nine strata"

Please identify **all** instances where references are cited in the main text, and extract (fact, ref_idx, url) triplets. When extracting, pay attention to the following:
1. Since these facts will need to be verified later, you may need to look for some context before and after the citation to ensure that the fact is complete and understandable, rather than just a simple phrase or short expression.
2. If a fact cites multiple references, then it should correspond to two triplets: (fact, ref_idx_1, url_1) and (fact, ref_idx_2, url_2).
3. For the third form of citation (i.e., where the citation source and link appear directly in the text), the ref_idx should be uniformly set to 0.
4. If the main text does not specify the exact location of the citation (for example, only the reference list is listed at the end of the article, without specifying the citation point in the text), please return an empty list.

You should return a JSON list format, where each item in the list is a triplet, for example:
[
    {{
        "fact": "Text segment from the original document. Note that Chinese quotation marks should use full-width marks. And add a single backslash before the English quotation mark to make it a readable for python json module.",
        "ref_idx": "The index of the cited reference in the reference list for this text segment.",
        "url": "The URL of the cited reference for this text segment (extracted from the reference list at the end of the research report or from the parentheses at the citation point)."
    }}
]

Here is the main text of the research report:
{report_text}

Please begin the extraction now. Output only the JSON list directly, without any chitchat or explanations."""


def clean_urls(input_text):
    # match [title](url) format
    pattern = re.compile(r'\[([^\]]+)\]\(([^)]+)\)')
    
    def repl(match):
        title = match.group(1)
        url = match.group(2)
        # truncate #:~:text= and its content
        cut_idx = url.find('#:~:text=')
        if cut_idx != -1:
            url = url[:cut_idx]
        return f'[{title}]({url})'
    
    return pattern.sub(repl, input_text)


def remove_urls(input_text):
    # match [title](url) format, only remove the content in the parentheses, keep [title]
    pattern = re.compile(r'\[([^\]]+)\]\(([^)]+)\)')
    # replace [title](url) with [title]
    return pattern.sub(r'[\1]', input_text)


def clean_escape(input_text):
    # replace illegal escape characters
    input_text = input_text.replace("\\>", ">")
    input_text = input_text.replace("\\<", "<")
    input_text = input_text.replace("\\+", "+")
    input_text = input_text.replace("\\~", "~")
    return input_text


def run(data, output_path, id_to_lang_map):
    for i, d in enumerate(data):
        # Determine the language based on the id in the query data
        article_id = d.get('id')
        
        if not article_id:
            print(f"Article has no ID field, skipping extraction")
            continue
            
        if article_id not in id_to_lang_map:
            print(f"Language not found for article ID: {article_id}")
            continue
            
        lang = id_to_lang_map[article_id]
        if lang not in ["zh", "en"]:
            print(f"Unsupported language: {lang} for article ID: {article_id}")
            continue
            
        # Select the prompt based on the language
        if lang == "zh":
            user_prompt = prompt_template.format(report_text=d['article'])
        elif lang == 'en':
            user_prompt = prompt_template_en.format(report_text=d['article'])
        
        response = call_model(user_prompt)
        
        retries = 0
        while retries < 3:
            retries += 1
            try:
                if response != "":
                    response = response.replace("```json", "").replace("```", "")
                    response = clean_escape(response)
                    
                    d['citations'] = json.loads(response)

                    for c in d['citations']:
                        c['fact'] = remove_urls(c['fact'])

                else:
                    d['citations'] = "extraction failed"

                with open(output_path, "a+", encoding='utf-8') as f:
                    f.write(json.dumps(d, ensure_ascii=False) + "\n")

                print(f">>>>>>>>>> generating {d['id']}-th instance...")
                break
            except Exception as e:
                print(repr(e))
                continue
        
        if 'citations' not in d:
            print(f">>>>>>>>>> All attempts failed, article ID: {article_id}, cannot extract citations")


if __name__ == '__main__':

    multiprocessing.set_start_method('fork')

    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--output_path", type=str, required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--raw_data_path", type=str, required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--query_data_path", type=str, required=True, help="Path to query data with language information")
    parser.add_argument("--n_total_process", type=int, default=1)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    output_path = args.output_path
    
    # Load the query data to get language information
    query_data = load_jsonl(args.query_data_path)
    
    # Create a mapping from ID to language
    id_to_lang_map = {item['id']: item.get('language') for item in query_data if 'id' in item and 'language' in item}
    
    if not id_to_lang_map:
        raise ValueError("No valid language information found in query data")
    
    # Load the raw data
    raw_data = load_jsonl(args.raw_data_path)

    # If the output file exists, load the processed ids and filter out the processed instances
    if os.path.exists(output_path):
        processed = [d['id'] for d in load_jsonl(output_path)]
        data_to_process = [d for d in raw_data if d['id'] not in processed]
    else:
        data_to_process = raw_data

    # OpenAI deep research will add webpage snippets to the citations.
    # For fair comparison, we remove these snippets
    if 'openai' in args.raw_data_path:
        for d in data_to_process:
            d['article'] = clean_urls(d['article'])

    print(f"Processing {len(data_to_process)} instances...")

    n_total_process = args.n_total_process
    if n_total_process == 1:
        run(data_to_process, output_path, id_to_lang_map)
    elif n_total_process > 1:
        part_size = (len(data_to_process) + n_total_process - 1) // n_total_process
        data_splits = [data_to_process[i * part_size : (i + 1) * part_size] for i in range(n_total_process)]
        run_partial = partial(run, output_path=output_path, id_to_lang_map=id_to_lang_map)
        with multiprocessing.Pool(processes=n_total_process) as pool:
            results = pool.map(run_partial, data_splits)

input.command:
f=/home/polaris/wt/faithoff/outputs/faithoff_t72/report.md
grep -c '^|' $f; grep -c '^\- \|^\* \|^[0-9]\+\. ' $f; grep -c '^#' $f; grep -c '^\*\*' $f
echo ---sentence-length---
python3 -c "
import re
t=open('$f').read()
body=t.split('## References')[0] if '## References' in t else t
body=re.sub(r'\[\d+\]','',body)
sents=re.split(r'(?<=[.!?]) ',body)
lens=[len(s.split()) for s in sents if len(s.split())>3]
import statistics
print('sentences',len(lens),'mean words/sent',round(statistics.mean(lens),1),'median',statistics.median(lens),'p90',sorted(lens)[int(.9*len(lens))])
paras=[p for p in body.split(chr(10)+chr(10)) if len(p.split())>10]
plens=[len(p.split()) for p in paras]
print('paragraphs',len(paras),'mean words/para',round(statistics.mean(plens),1),'max',max(plens))
"
grep -n "^## \|^### " $f | head -30

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""STEP 15: compose the REAL scoreable multi-section report from the LIVE agentic run.
3	
4	The mission's metric-(a) full-corpus gate (cp4_used=agentic on the 329-basket corpus) already
5	PASSED (docs/agentic_sweep_live_summary_s3gear329.json). What was missing was a *composed*
6	report we can score. This driver closes that gap: it runs the FULL generator
7	(``generate_multi_section_report``) with the agentic outliner ON (PG_OUTLINE_AGENT=1 + the
8	§9.1.8 model lock) over data/cp4_corpus_s3gear_329.json and writes report.md.
9	
10	Model it on scripts/run_honest_on_prerebuild_corpus.py (which already produced report.md +

14	[CITE:ev_xxx] token in the composed report. The strict_verify lane already enforces this
15	per-section; this driver re-audits the final assembled text as an independent tripwire.

17	Run (key MUST be in env):
18	    set -a && . ./.env && set +a
19	    PG_OUTLINE_AGENT=1 python scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py \
20	        --corpus data/cp4_corpus_s3gear_329.json

48	def _derive_title(rq: str) -> str:
49	    """Derive a neutral report title from the research question — GENERAL, not tuned to any task.
50	
51	    Takes the first sentence/clause of the RQ, strips a leading imperative ("Please write a ...",
52	    "Research ...", "I am researching ..."), and Title-cases nothing (keeps the RQ's own wording).
53	    Falls back to a generic label. No topic is hardcoded."""
54	    import re as _re
55	    s = (rq or "").strip().replace("\n", " ")
56	    s = _re.sub(r"\s+", " ", s)
57	    # First sentence only.
58	    s = _re.split(r"(?<=[.?!])\s", s, maxsplit=1)[0]
59	    # Strip common leading imperatives so the title reads as a subject, not a command.
60	    s = _re.sub(r"^(please\s+)?(help me\s+)?(write|prepare|produce|conduct|research(ing)?|"
61	                r"provide|create|complete|collect( and)?( organi[sz]e)?|i am researching|"
62	                r"i would like|i need)\b[:,]?\s*", "", s, flags=_re.IGNORECASE)
63	    s = s.strip().rstrip(".").strip()
64	    if not s:
65	        return "Research Report"
66	    # Capitalize the first letter only (preserve proper-noun casing in the rest).
67	    return s[0].upper() + s[1:]

71	    """Load a DeepResearch-Bench task's EXACT prompt verbatim (target/ref/criteria all key on it)."""

73	        o = json.loads(line)
74	        if str(o.get("id")) == str(task_id):
75	            return o["prompt"]
76	    raise SystemExit(f"BLOCKED: DRB task id {task_id} not in {DRB_QUERY}")

89	def _tier_fractions(evidence: list[dict]) -> dict[str, float]:
90	    from collections import Counter
91	    c = Counter((e.get("tier") or "T?").upper() for e in evidence)
92	    n = sum(c.values()) or 1
93	    return {k: v / n for k, v in sorted(c.items())}

96	# A numeric token that would be a faithfulness breach if it sat inside a [CITE:] sentence
97	# without having passed strict_verify. We audit the FINAL assembled report: any [CITE:ev_xxx]
98	# in the verified text is, by construction, already span-grounded — but we re-scan to prove it.
99	_CITE_RE = re.compile(r"\[CITE:(ev_[0-9a-fA-F]+|[a-z0-9_]+)\]")
100	
101	
102	def _audit_citations(report_text: str, biblio: list[dict]) -> dict:
103	    """Independent faithfulness tripwire on the FINAL assembled report.
104	
105	    strict_verify resolves every kept sentence's provenance token into a global [N] bibliography
106	    marker and DROPS any sentence whose number failed the span match. So in a faithful final
107	    report: (1) ZERO raw [CITE:ev_xxx] tokens survive (any survivor is an unverified-number leak
108	    — the exact breach the mission forbids), and (2) every [N] marker in the prose resolves to a
109	    real bibliography entry. We assert both."""
110	    leaked_cites = _CITE_RE.findall(report_text)
111	    body = report_text.split("\n\n## References\n", 1)[0]  # markers in prose only
112	    n_markers = set(int(m) for m in re.findall(r"\[(\d+)\]", body))
113	    biblio_nums = {int(b.get("num")) for b in biblio if str(b.get("num", "")).isdigit()}
114	    unresolved = sorted(n for n in n_markers if n not in biblio_nums)
115	    return {
116	        "leaked_cite_ev_tokens": len(leaked_cites),
117	        "leaked_cite_samples": sorted(set(leaked_cites))[:10],
118	        "distinct_bib_markers_in_prose": len(n_markers),
119	        "bibliography_entries": len(biblio_nums),
120	        "unresolved_markers": unresolved,
121	    }
122	
123	
124	def _order_sections_by_required(verified: list, required_titles: list[str]) -> list:
125	    """Reorder VERIFIED sections so those matching the required titles come first,
126	    in the required order; all others keep their original relative order after.
127	
128	    Matching is case-insensitive containment (a required title matched against a
129	    produced heading). ORDER-ONLY: every input section is present in the output
130	    exactly once — nothing is dropped or fabricated. A required title with no
131	    matching verified section is simply skipped (disclosure is the audit's job)."""
132	    want = [(t or "").strip().lower() for t in required_titles if (t or "").strip()]
133	    used: set[int] = set()
134	    ordered: list = []
135	    for w in want:
136	        for i, sr in enumerate(verified):
137	            if i in used:
138	                continue
139	            tl = (sr.title or "").strip().lower()
140	            if tl == w or w in tl or tl in w:
141	                ordered.append(sr)
142	                used.add(i)
143	                break
144	    for i, sr in enumerate(verified):
145	        if i not in used:
146	            ordered.append(sr)
147	    return ordered
148	
149	
150	def _dedup_biblio_by_work(biblio: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
151	    """Collapse bibliography rows that resolve to the same underlying WORK.
152	
153	    Key is the normalized URL when present, else the normalized statement text.
154	    The FIRST row for each work is kept (preserving its assigned number); later
155	    duplicates are removed. This drops only DUPLICATE reference lines — never a
156	    distinct source — and does not touch in-prose [N] markers."""
157	    seen: set[str] = set()
158	    out: list[dict] = []
159	    for b in biblio:
160	        url = str(b.get("url", "") or "").strip().lower().rstrip("/")
161	        key = url or str(b.get("statement", "") or "").strip().lower()[:160]
162	        if key and key in seen:
163	            continue
164	        if key:
165	            seen.add(key)
166	        out.append(b)
167	    return out

202	    # verdict-identity A/B), PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY in the 4-8 band (NEVER >=48), and

422	        ]
423	        # S4 contract-aware order: when the render plan names required section titles
424	        # in order, place matching verified sections FIRST in that order; every other
425	        # verified section keeps its original relative order AFTER them. This ORDERS
426	        # verified content — it never drops or fabricates a section. Absent render
427	        # plan => original order => byte-identical.
428	        _required_titles = list(_render_plan.get("required_titles", [])) if _render_plan else []
429	        if _required_titles:
430	            _verified = _order_sections_by_required(_verified, _required_titles)
431	        bodies: list[str] = [f"## {sr.title}\n\n{sr.verified_text}" for sr in _verified]
432	        sections_concat = "\n\n".join(bodies)
433	        if getattr(multi, "limitations_text", ""):
434	            sections_concat += f"\n\n## Limitations\n\n{multi.limitations_text}"
435	
436	        biblio = getattr(multi, "bibliography", []) or []
437	        # S4 references dedup by WORK: when the render plan requests it (default True
438	        # in a loaded contract), collapse bibliography rows that resolve to the same
439	        # underlying work (same url, else same statement). The FIRST row's number is
440	        # kept; this only removes DUPLICATE reference lines, never a distinct source,
441	        # and does not renumber in-prose markers. No gate artifact => biblio unchanged.
442	        _dedup_by_work = bool(_render_plan.get("references_dedup_by_work")) if _render_plan else False
443	        biblio_render = _dedup_biblio_by_work(biblio) if _dedup_by_work else biblio
444	        biblio_section = "\n\n## References\n"
445	        for b in biblio_render:
446	            biblio_section += (f"[{b.get('num')}] {str(b.get('statement',''))[:200]} — "
447	                               f"{b.get('url','')} (tier {b.get('tier','')})\n")
448	
449	        final_report = (f"# {title}\n\n{intro}\n\n{sections_concat}{biblio_section}")
450	        (run_dir / "report.md").write_text(final_report, encoding="utf-8")
451	
452	        # Pipeline telemetry / Methods is a SIDECAR artifact (provenance for us), NOT part of the judged
453	        # deliverable — a research report's reader does not want the generator's internal telemetry.
454	        tier_summary = ", ".join(f"{k}={v*100:.0f}%" for k, v in sorted(dist.items()))
455	        methods = (
456	            "# Methods / pipeline telemetry (sidecar — NOT part of the judged report.md)\n\n"
457	            f"Judged task: DRB task {args.rq_drb_task} (verbatim prompt).\n"
458	            f"Corpus RQ (provenance): {corpus_rq[:200]}...\n"
459	            f"Corpus: {corpus_path.name} ({len(evidence)} evidence rows, {len(clusters)} baskets; "
460	            f"domain={domain or 'general'}).\n"
461	            f"Outliner: AGENTIC (PG_OUTLINE_AGENT=1) — agent {outliner_agent_model()}, "
462	            f"code {outliner_code_model()}.\n"
463	            f"Generator: {PG_GENERATOR_MODEL} (multi-section: agentic outline + "
464	            f"{len(kept)} parallel verified sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\n"
465	            f"Evaluator/mirror: {PG_EVALUATOR_MODEL}.\n"
466	            f"Tier distribution: {tier_summary}.\n"
467	        )
468	        (run_dir / "methods.md").write_text(methods, encoding="utf-8")
469	        (run_dir / "bibliography.json").write_text(
470	            json.dumps(biblio, indent=2, sort_keys=True) + "\n", encoding="utf-8")
471	
472	        # P0/proof: the agentic-outliner digest surfaced on MultiSectionResult — PROVE the deep render
473	        # stayed agentic (cp4_used='agentic'), NOT degraded-to-seed (mission metric-1).
474	        oa_stats = dict(getattr(multi, "outline_agent_stats", None) or {})
475	        cp4_used = str(oa_stats.get("cp4_used", "MISSING"))
476	        degraded_to_seed = bool(oa_stats.get("degraded_to_seed", False))
477	        degrade_reason = str(oa_stats.get("degrade_reason", ""))
478	        log.info("[agentic] cp4_used=%s degraded_to_seed=%s turns=%s degrade_reason=%r -> %s",
479	                 cp4_used, degraded_to_seed, oa_stats.get("turns"), degrade_reason[:160],
480	                 "AGENTIC" if cp4_used == "agentic" else "NOT-AGENTIC")
481	
482	        audit = _audit_citations(final_report, biblio)
483	        faithful = (audit["leaked_cite_ev_tokens"] == 0 and not audit["unresolved_markers"])
484	        log.info("[faithfulness] leaked_[CITE:ev]=%d  bib_markers_in_prose=%d  bib_entries=%d  "
485	                 "unresolved_markers=%s -> %s",
486	                 audit["leaked_cite_ev_tokens"], audit["distinct_bib_markers_in_prose"],
487	                 audit["bibliography_entries"], audit["unresolved_markers"],
488	                 "PASS" if faithful else "FAIL")
489	
490	        # S4 contract-compliance audit — DISCLOSURE-ONLY, ALONGSIDE (never in place of)
491	        # the citation audit above (which is untouched). It reads the finished report +
492	        # outline + biblio and reports term-level SATISFIED/FAILED/UNSATISFIABLE/UNKNOWN
493	        # per contract term with its owning stage. It NEVER drops/edits content and NEVER
494	        # touches strict_verify. retrieval_scope_status is recorded as
495	        # 'not_evaluated_prebuilt_corpus' because this driver runs on a PREBUILT corpus —
496	        # discovery never ran under the contract, so no scope requirement is claimed
497	        # satisfied. No --gate-artifact => no contract => an empty audit (fail-open).
498	        compliance = None
499	        if _gate_contract is not None:
500	            try:
501	                from src.polaris_graph.planning.contract_compliance import (  # noqa: PLC0415
502	                    audit_contract,
503	                )
504	                outline_titles = [s.title for s in multi.sections
505	                                  if not s.dropped_due_to_failure and s.verified_text]
506	                _ca = audit_contract(
507	                    _gate_contract,
508	                    final_report,
509	                    outline=outline_titles,
510	                    biblio=biblio,
511	                    retrieval_scope_status="not_evaluated_prebuilt_corpus",
512	                    contract_sha256=_contract_sha,
513	                )
514	                compliance = _ca.to_dict()
515	                (run_dir / "contract_compliance.json").write_text(
516	                    json.dumps(compliance, indent=2) + "\n", encoding="utf-8")
517	                log.info("[compliance] counts=%s retrieval_scope_status=%s -> "
518	                         "contract_compliance.json",
519	                         compliance.get("counts"), compliance.get("retrieval_scope_status"))
520	            except Exception as _e:  # noqa: BLE001 — disclosure-only, never fails the run
521	                log.warning("[compliance] audit_contract failed (%s); skipping disclosure "
522	                            "(faithfulness + citation audit unaffected)", _e)
523	                compliance = None
524	
525	        summary = {
526	            "corpus": corpus_path.name,
527	            "judged_drb_task": args.rq_drb_task or None,
528	            "composed_to_rq": rq[:160],
529	            "corpus_rq": corpus_rq[:160],
530	            "report_title": title,
531	            "section_headings": [s.title for s in multi.sections
532	                                 if not s.dropped_due_to_failure and s.verified_text],
533	            "evidence_rows": len(evidence),
534	            "baskets": len(clusters),
535	            "same_work_groups": len(swg or []),
536	            "outline_sections": len(multi.outline),
537	            "kept_sections": len(kept),
538	            "dropped_sections": len(multi.sections) - len(kept),
539	            "total_words": getattr(multi, "total_words", None),
540	            "total_sentences_verified": getattr(multi, "total_sentences_verified", None),
541	            "total_sentences_dropped": getattr(multi, "total_sentences_dropped", None),
542	            "bibliography_entries": len(biblio),
543	            "report_chars": len(final_report),
544	            "report_words": len(final_report.split()),
545	            "faithfulness_audit": audit,
546	            "faithfulness_pass": faithful,
547	            # S4 disclosure: retrieval scope was NOT evaluated (prebuilt corpus). The
548	            # contract-compliance audit (when a gate artifact was supplied) is a
549	            # SIDECAR disclosure — it never gates the run's return code.
550	            "retrieval_scope_status": "not_evaluated_prebuilt_corpus",
551	            "contract_compliance": compliance,
552	            "cp4_used": cp4_used,
553	            "degraded_to_seed": degraded_to_seed,
554	            "degrade_reason": degrade_reason[:200],
555	            "outline_agent_turns": oa_stats.get("turns"),
556	            "moat_quantified_models": len(getattr(multi, "quantified_models", None) or {}),
557	            "agent_model": outliner_agent_model(),
558	            "code_model": outliner_code_model(),
559	            "generator_model": PG_GENERATOR_MODEL,
560	            "elapsed_seconds": round(dt, 1),
561	            "out_dir": str(run_dir),
562	        }
563	        (run_dir / "compose_summary.json").write_text(
564	            json.dumps(summary, indent=2) + "\n", encoding="utf-8")
565	        log.info("WROTE %s (%d chars, %d words) + compose_summary.json",
566	                 run_dir / "report.md", len(final_report), len(final_report.split()))
567	        print(json.dumps(summary, indent=2))
568	        return 0 if faithful else 1
569	    finally:
570	        # LOGGING FIX: clear the run-scoped sink so it cannot leak into a reused process.
571	        set_reasoning_sink(None)
572	
573	
574	if __name__ == "__main__":
575	    raise SystemExit(asyncio.run(main()))
576
